search_summary.key_finding: pytidymodels2 repository does not exist. No Python implementation of tidymodels-style workflows was found with this name. Tidymodels is an R-specific framework.

repositories.architecture: Repository does not exist. Search results only returned tidymodels (R framework) and no Python equivalent.
limitations: Repository does not exist or may be private/unlisted

built_in_charts.description: Visualizes relationships between multiple parameters and metrics
built_in_charts.use_case: Understanding effect of parameter settings on model performance

built_in_charts.description: Shows relationship between a parameter (X-axis) and metric (Y-axis)
built_in_charts.use_case: Identifying trends and correlations between variables

built_in_charts.description: Visualizes relationship between two parameters and a scalar metric
built_in_charts.use_case: How learning rate and batch size affect model accuracy

built_in_charts.description: Shows metric history over training steps/epochs
built_in_charts.use_case: Monitoring training progress (loss curves, validation metrics)

limitations: Cannot create fully custom dashboard layouts

mlflow_assistant.description: AI-powered in-product chatbot that helps identify, diagnose, and fix issues

agents_dashboard.description: New Overview tab for GenAI experiments with pre-built charts
features: Chat sessions tab for conversational workflows

judge_builder_ui.description: UI for creating and testing LLM judges for quality evaluation
capabilities: Pre-defined judges for safety, relevance, groundedness, correctness

limitations: Limited visual analytics for model metrics

approaches.description: Define custom metrics in evaluation framework
Log interactive Plotly/Bokeh charts as HTML artifacts
approaches.limitation: Not integrated into main UI, viewed separately

approaches.description: Query experiment data via API and visualize in Plotly Dash, Metabase, or BI tools

gaps_and_limitations.citation: Multiple sources including Valohai Blog and MLflow GitHub Issues, 2026
collaboration: Basic RBAC (READ, EDIT, MANAGE, NO_PERMISSIONS only)
No project isolation or team boundaries
Difficult to share experiments at scale
scalability: Manual processes don't scale beyond small teams
Limited organizational features for large teams
Performance issues with large numbers of experiments
visualization: Cannot visualize aggregated trace metrics easily
data_management: Focuses on model tracking over data tracking

typical_features.citation: Plotly. 'Machine Learning Dash App Examples.' Plotly, 2026. https://plotly.com/examples/machine-learning/

model_performance_visualizations.description: Receiver Operating Characteristic curves with AUC scores
Classification results matrix with various formats
Model performance vs training set size

experiment_comparison.description: Custom tables with filtering, sorting, and conditional formatting
experiment_comparison.advantages_over_mlflow: Full control over columns, calculations, and styling

experiment_comparison.description: Heatmaps showing parameter combinations vs performance
experiment_comparison.advantages_over_mlflow: More visualization options than parallel coordinates

experiment_comparison.description: Experiment performance over time with trend lines
experiment_comparison.advantages_over_mlflow: Not available in MLflow by default
Statistical rigor not built into MLflow

interactive_capabilities.description: Input form + instant prediction with explanation
Select 2+ models and compare on same data point
Analyze model performance by data segments
Click on any data point to see details
Interactive slider to adjust classification thresholds

business_intelligence_features.description: Business-specific metrics calculated from ML outputs

advanced_visualizations.use_case: Model pipeline flows and data transformations
Hierarchical model structure or error analysis
Show training progress or time-based changes

advantages: Rich ecosystem of UI libraries (Material-UI, Ant Design)
Better state management for complex interactions (Redux, MobX)
typical_implementations: Integration with MLflow backend via REST API

advantages: Tighter integration with data science libraries
Easy to deploy as standalone app
limitations: Less flexible than React for complex UIs

mlflow_strengths: Zero setup for basic experiment tracking
Good for team collaboration on experiments
custom_dashboard_strengths: Complete control over visualizations and layout
custom_dashboard_gaps: Need to build experiment tracking infrastructure
Need to implement security and access control
Data storage and querying infrastructure needed
Version control and change management complexity

common_patterns: MLflow as backend + custom frontend (hybrid approach)
Use MLflow API to query experiment data, visualize in Dash/React
Log custom HTML artifacts (Plotly charts) to MLflow
Databricks system tables → BI tool integration
Separate dashboards for different audiences (technical vs executive)
best_practices: Start with MLflow UI for rapid prototyping
Identify visualization gaps before building custom dashboards
Use MLflow's artifact storage for custom visualizations
Build custom dashboards iteratively (start with highest-value features)
Use hybrid approach: MLflow for tracking + custom UI for analysis
Leverage MLflow's REST API for integration
pitfalls: Building custom dashboards too early (premature optimization)
Recreating features that MLflow already provides
Not planning for scalability from the start
Ignoring security and access control requirements
Over-engineering visualizations that stakeholders don't use
emerging_trends: AI-powered assistants in ML platforms (MLflow Assistant)
Real-time trace analysis for agent systems
Continuous quality monitoring with automated judges

implementation_recommendations.scenario: Small team (< 5 people), standard ML workflows
implementation_recommendations.rationale: Built-in features sufficient, no custom development needed, faster time-to-value

implementation_recommendations.scenario: Medium team (5-20 people), need model explainability
implementation_recommendations.recommended_solution: MLflow + logged HTML artifacts (SHAP plots as HTML)
implementation_recommendations.rationale: Leverage MLflow infrastructure, add explainability without full custom dashboard

implementation_recommendations.recommended_solution: MLflow backend + custom Plotly Dash dashboard
implementation_recommendations.rationale: Python-only development, data scientists can maintain it, good balance of power and simplicity

implementation_recommendations.recommended_solution: MLflow backend + React dashboard + BI tool integration
implementation_recommendations.rationale: Professional UI, business metrics, executive dashboards, scalable architecture

implementation_recommendations.recommended_solution: MLflow + Jupyter notebooks + occasional Dash apps
implementation_recommendations.rationale: Flexibility for exploratory analysis, ad-hoc visualizations as needed

implementation_recommendations.recommended_solution: MLflow + dedicated monitoring platform (Grafana, Datadog) + custom prediction UI
implementation_recommendations.rationale: Separation of concerns: experiments (MLflow) vs production monitoring vs user-facing tools

implementation_recommendations.recommended_solution: MLflow 3.9+ with Agents Dashboard + custom evaluation interfaces
implementation_recommendations.rationale: Leverage new trace analysis, judge builder, and chat sessions features; add custom analysis for domain-specific evaluations

popular_solutions: Hybrid approach: MLflow for storage + custom visualization
Weights & Biases as alternative with better built-in UI
Neptune.ai for teams needing more advanced features
controversial_topics: Whether to build custom vs buy commercial MLOps platform
MLflow's limited RBAC and team features
Open source MLflow vs Databricks Managed MLflow
When custom dashboards justify the development cost
expert_opinions: MLflow excels at experiment tracking but needs augmentation for production monitoring
Custom dashboards most valuable for stakeholder communication, not data science work
Invest in custom visualization only after MLflow limitations are clearly identified
For LLM applications, MLflow 3.9+ significantly closes the gap with specialized features
